text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and inserts robotic instruments. The operation begins with the dissection of omental adhesions, revealing a bulbous gallbladder remnant and an adjacent tubular common bile duct. Endocyanin reinjection is used to enhance visualization of these structures.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, cystic duct, and gallbladder remnant. The peritoneum overlying the gallbladder remnant is scored, with additional scoring posteriorly along the infundibulum of the gallbladder. Dissection continues to expose the insertion of the cystic duct, with anterior scoring along the liver's edge.

The surgeon identifies a tubular structure as the cystic duct, confirmed using ICG and Firefly technology. Careful dissection delineates the junction between the cystic duct and gallbladder remnant. The anatomy is repeatedly checked to avoid injury to the common bile duct. The cystic duct is encircled and transected between robotic plastic clips.

The surgeon performs a final check before transecting the cystic duct, dissecting it off the liver bed while staying close to the gallbladder edge. The most adherent part is at the fundal end, where old laparoscopic clips are discovered. A large hemlock clip from the previous cholecystectomy is visible. The entire gallbladder remnant is placed in an endoscopic retrieval bag for removal.
The surgeon inspects the operative bed, confirming hemostasis and the absence of bile leakage. The common bile duct (CBD) is intact, with no visible bile leakage.
